nativeRange.habitat: Riparian and marshland environments — typically grows emergent along the margins of streams, ponds, and rice paddies, but tolerates long periods fully submerged

decorPreferences: background planting area with vertical space for tall stems
nutrient-rich substrate preferred but tolerates plain gravel or sand
best displayed in dense groups of multiple stems

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large herbivorous cichlids

summary: Hygrophila corymbosa (Temple Plant, Giant Hygro) is a large, fast-growing background stem plant from Southeast Asia with broad bright-green leaves. Generally regarded as one of the easiest beginner stem plants — undemanding on light, CO2, and substrate, but needs frequent trimming.
careNotes: Hygrophila corymbosa is a hardy, fast-growing stem plant well suited to beginners and low-tech tanks. Reaches a substantial size — generally 30-50+ cm tall under typical aquarium conditions — and is best used as a background or tall midground plant in groups of several stems. Frequent pruning is generally advised both to control height and to encourage lateral branching for a denser stand. Sources disagree on light demand: Tropica describes it as low-demand, Modern Aquarium and Wikipedia as moderate, and Flowgrow as medium-to-high; a medium-light setup is a reasonable middle-ground that supports healthy growth without forcing excessive maintenance. CO2 is not strictly required and the plant tolerates non-injected tanks, but supplemental CO2 (around 10-40 mg/l per Flowgrow) noticeably improves growth rate and leaf size. Substrate is flexible — plain gravel or sand will work, though nutrient-rich aquasoil combined with regular liquid fertilization gives fuller, healthier growth. Iron supplementation is reported to help if leaves begin to yellow. Tolerates a wide temperature range (Flowgrow and Modern Aquarium report tolerance from ~18 to 30°C); the 22-28°C window above reflects the commonly recommended aquarium range. Numerous named cultivars and trade forms exist: 'Compacta' (a more compact, bushier form often placed in the midground), 'Stricta' / 'Angustifolia' (narrow-leaved forms, sometimes treated as the separate species Hygrophila angustifolia), 'Aroma' (a scented variant), and 'Siamensis' / 'Thai'. Wikipedia notes that many of these are mainly ecological variants whose appearance depends on lighting and iron availability. Minimum tank volume of 75 L is an estimated guideline reflecting the plant's adult height; no source specified an exact figure. Useful for nutrient export — has been reported to take up ammonium and nitrate readily, helping suppress algae in well-planted tanks.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings: trim a healthy top or lateral shoot 10-15 cm long, remove the lowest pair of leaves, and replant directly into the substrate. New roots generally form quickly. Regular topping and replanting of cuttings maintains a dense, bushy stand and is the standard way of rejuvenating older stems whose bases have lost leaves.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Tropica describes Hygrophila corymbosa as an easy starter plant with low light/CO2 demand, fast growth, 20-30+ cm height after two months, and propagation by stem cuttings.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked the 'Compacta' variant: temperature 64-86°F (18-30°C), moderate light, moderate-to-fast growth, midground placement, propagation by cuttings, easy difficulty, India origin.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Lamiales), full tolerance ranges (temperature 18-30°C, pH 5.5-7.5, kH 2-18°dKH), CO2 10-40 mg/l, fast growth, medium-to-high light, and synonymy with Hygrophila stricta.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for common names (Temple Plant, Starhorn, Giant Hygro), native range (Malaysia, Thailand, Singapore, Indonesia, India), riparian/emergent habitat, cultivar/form information, and ammonium/nitrate uptake note.
